title: operations path placement
description: A short quiz on the core ideas of the Operations path. Answer what you know. Each chapter you place out of is one you can skip ahead on, and you can revisit any chapter any time.

scoring.chapterSkipRule: Each chapter is tested by 2 questions worth 2 points each. A learner places out of a chapter (skips its Version A) by answering both of its questions correctly. Overall, 50 percent or more of maxScore recommends Version B as the default for chapters not individually skipped.

questions.prompt: In a terminal, what does the cd command do?

options.label: Changes which folder you are currently in
Copies a file
Deletes a folder
Closes the terminal

questions.prompt: In a terminal, what does the ls command do?

options.label: Lists what is inside the folder you are currently in
Logs you out
Loads a saved file
Locks the screen

questions.prompt: You need to run the same three-step process on every vendor in a list of 200. What tool fits?

options.label: A loop, which repeats the same steps once for each item in the list
Writing the three steps out 200 times by hand
Doing it for one vendor and hoping the rest sort themselves out
A single variable
open, then open, then closed, each on its own line
open, then closed (duplicates are skipped)
status, status, status

questions.prompt: What is error handling, in plain terms?

options.label: Code that catches a failure and decides what to do, instead of letting the program crash
Code that guarantees nothing ever goes wrong
A way to hide errors so nobody notices
A type of error message

questions.prompt: A nightly automation processes 1,000 records and hits one bad record. What is the well-designed behavior?

options.label: Catch the failure on that record, note it, and keep processing the other 999
Crash the whole job and process nothing
Silently skip the bad record and leave no record that it happened
Delete all 1,000 records to be safe

questions.prompt: You want an automation that reads each incoming request and classifies it with an AI model, on its own. What makes that possible?

options.label: Calling an AI model through its API from code, so each request triggers the model
A person pasting every request into a chat window
Nothing, classification cannot be automated
A spreadsheet with no formulas

questions.prompt: Your automation will call an AI model thousands of times a day. What belongs in your thinking?

options.label: Each call has a cost and a small chance of a wrong result, so you budget and add checks
Calls are free, instant, and always correct at any scale
The model gets cheaper the more you call it
Nothing needs planning

questions.prompt: An automation gets a request, looks up a record, checks a status, and then takes an action based on what it found, repeating until done. What is this pattern called?

options.label: An agent loop: it takes a step, uses what it learns, and continues until the task is finished
A single fixed formula
A printout
A keyboard shortcut

questions.prompt: What can an agent loop do that a single one-shot step cannot?

options.label: React to what it finds at each step and adjust the next step accordingly
Nothing, they are identical
Run with no instructions at all
Avoid ever needing review
